original_abstract: This article examines the enduring health and social consequences of United States nuclear weapons testing in Micronesia, specifically focusing on the Marshall Islands. Between 1946 and 1958, the U.S. detonated 66 nuclear devices in the region, including the massive Castle Bravo test, which resulted in widespread radioactive fallout. The authors argue that U.S. government research through "Project 4.1" involved significant human rights violations, including the deliberate exposure of populations to radiation for scientific study and subsequent medical experimentation without informed consent. The U.S. government’s current estimates of radiation-induced cancers are criticized as being mathematically modeled to minimize the true extent of the harm, ignoring localized environmental contamination and reproductive abnormalities reported by residents. Beyond direct oncogenic effects, the legacy of testing is linked to profound social disruptions, including forced displacement and a transition to sedentary lifestyles and processed diets, which have fueled an epidemic of obesity and diabetes. The paper concludes by calling for the U.S. to declassify all data regarding fallout patterns, urging the reinstatement of Medicaid eligibility for Compact of Free Association (COFA) migrants as a matter of social justice, and supporting international legal efforts to pursue nuclear disarmament.
humanized_abstract: Atomic Legacies A Review of the Literature on Health and Social Impacts on Compact Island Nations from U.S. Nuclear Testings in Micronesia – With Particular Reference to the Marshall Islands* 1 Introduction U.S. nuclear weapons testing in the Pacific led to fallout landing anddrifting in the Marshall Islands; several hundred nuclear tests including the most powerful atomic bomb, (code named) “Castle Bravo”, the so-called “Superbomb” detonated in March 1954; 66 nuclear bombs were dropped. The Marshall Islanders are still affected by harmful U.S. weapons experiments that fall far short of upholding basic human rights and the basic right to live. Indeed many rights abuses and violations have occurred from work done in the so-called U.S. government Project 4.1 conducted on Marshall Islanders before testing of the atomic bomb took place and soon thereafter by doctors using tissue samples. These have all obviously come about through the testing of Atomic bombs over a twelve-year-period between 1946 and 1958. U.S. government findings for rates of cancer as being associated with Atomic Legacy by Hiro S . Tanaka, Kaya B. Shirley, Yoshinori Yasuda: University of Hawaii Institute for Energy Research and Security - This paper uses new analyses for cancer causing radiation dosages for calculations found with U.S. Government studies with regards to exposure by Fallout by testing of weapons at Pacific Proving grounds. These studies give minimum or lowest bounds on dosages used with the full knowledge of all forms of contamination on the Islands. A large numbers of contaminate local sites at the Islands with levels highly exceeding government minimum estimates have not been measured in official documentation at local sites at the Islands where their prevalence is known to all residents on the Atolls that they tested with radiation falling from U.S. bombings that are known to be 1,000 fold or 1000 time greater at a smaller area of contaminated sites found on outer Islands for more populated and urban atolls having higher concentrations than documented officially. There are thousands of records of more radiation injury associated to several types of birth defects found locally without official records. Also many radiation related damage in various internal reproductive organ functions on atolls in Marshall Island have been reported that have been denied entry for adequate care using national Medicaid requirements for migrants under terms agreed to in the Compact of Free Association and also needs redress by the new U.S. congress since these have been known to all local governments to all inhabitants over a long period of time, some three decades and more and thus constitutes sufficient information that may make impossible ever achieving and fulfilling goals to make totally safe the use of nuclear arms which all countries that agree for weapons to be banned should demand or hold hostage for disarmament of existing weapons while goals and more in-sights towards this goal can come by fully disclosing all documents and sites that fallout came upon over periods long past which can lead to answers by having a full and fair international accounting which all citizens could begin to have better trust and have assurance about nuclear arms being on the path to safer goal.
